segments.title: Pre-war destroyer procurement as a preparedness problem
segments.summary: The video opens with Mahan and preparedness, using the delay in creating war material as the frame for pre-WWI destroyer procurement.
segments.body: Ships cannot be postponed until hostilities are imminent, especially under modern construction timelines.

evidence.note: Opening cites Mahan and preparedness before connecting the argument to destroyer debate and naval construction timing.

segments.title: Mahan and the destroyer debate
segments.summary: The opening uses Mahan on preparedness to frame why destroyers matter before war begins.
segments.body: The discussion places the destroyer issue inside a broader fleet and preparedness argument.

evidence.note: The opening uses Mahan on preparedness to frame why destroyers matter before war begins.

segments.title: Battleship funding leaves destroyers scarce
segments.summary: The transcript contrasts battleship construction with much smaller destroyer procurement.
segments.body: The discussion separates hull-count priorities from the opening policy frame.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts battleship construction with much smaller destroyer procurement.

segments.title: Wartime destroyer building does not solve preparedness
segments.summary: The episode argues that large World War I destroyer output came too late to count as pre-war preparedness.
segments.body: This point preserves the distinction between wartime mobilization and having forces ready.

evidence.note: The episode argues that large World War I destroyer output came too late to count as pre-war preparedness.

segments.title: Flotilla defence evolves before World War II
segments.summary: The middle section follows how destroyer roles evolve into flotilla defence across the interwar period.
segments.body: The discussion links the pre-WWI scarcity problem to later doctrinal development.

evidence.note: The middle section follows how destroyer roles evolve into flotilla defence across the interwar period.

segments.title: Too few destroyers also have to act as scouts
segments.summary: The late section argues that scarce US destroyers were expected to double as scouts because cruiser numbers were also thin.
segments.body: This shows why destroyer scarcity affected more than torpedo or escort roles.

evidence.note: The late section argues that scarce US destroyers were expected to double as scouts because cruiser numbers were also thin.

segments.title: Useful destroyer force conclusion
segments.summary: The conclusion emphasizes that good destroyers still need useful numbers to provide an effective force.
segments.body: This closes the episode around the gap between ship quality and force quantity.

evidence.note: The conclusion emphasizes that good destroyers still need useful numbers to provide an effective force.
